content.full_text:
CITY OF HAYWARD

FPPC
Office of the City Attorney
MAR 19 12 36 PM '93

March 16, 1993


Ms. Jill Stecher
Counsel, Legal Division
California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95812

Re:  Letter No. 92-613  497

Dear Ms. Stecher:

     Kerwin Quon has asked me to follow up on my letter of
February 23, 1993, to see whether the material I provided
satisfied your request for supplemental information.  Are you at
this time able to determine whether or not the Human Services
Commission is an advisory body?  Please let me know when he might
expect your formal opinion or if there is some further
information you need.

     Thank you for your assistance in this matter.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

David A. Weingard
Assistant City Attorney

DAW:cj


25151 Clawiter Road, Hayward, CA 94545-2731 • (415) 293-5530 • FAX (415) 293-1580 • TDD (415) 293-1590

Printed on recycled paper

CITY OF HAYWARD
OFFICE OF CITY ATTORNEY

June 1, 1992

TO:     Members of Advisory Bodies Subject to Conflict of Interest
        Rules

FROM:   City Attorney

RE:     Conflict of Interest Advice


     Some members of Hayward's various boards, commissions,
committees, and task forces never have to address conflict of
interest issues.  This is because of the nature of the items that
come before them or because they have no economic interests that
could create a potential conflict of interest.  Other members of
boards and commissions face such questions frequently and routinely.
This memo is addressed principally to those advisory body members
who have to deal with conflict of interest issues with some
frequency, but I encourage all members of the City's various
advisory bodies to read it.

     The Fair Political Practices Commission has taken a dramatic
new approach to local elected officials' ability to receive timely
legal advice on conflict-of-interest issues.  The bottom line is
that the Commission wants to give such advice, rather than local
agencies' city attorneys, county counsels, and similar agency-
affiliated lawyers.  The reason for this is the Commission's belief
that local attorneys misinterpret the law because they are too
easily pressured to give erroneous legal advice when they are
dealing with their own bosses.

     To keep local attorneys from giving this "pressured" advice,
the Commission has adopted a position that attempts to discourage
use of local attorneys in favor of seeking its staff attorney's
advice.  The method of accomplishing this has two parts.  The first
is by requiring each local attorney to write a formal opinion which
reflects that the attorney has sought and received all relevant
factual information from the local official and then researched the
statutes, regulations, and Commission opinions and advice letters.
The second is by not providing immunity from prosecution if a local
official acts in reliance on his or her local attorney's advice, but
providing that immunity if he or she acts in compliance with an FPPC
staff attorney's advice.  Since it can easily take up to 30 days or
more to seek an opinion from the FPPC staff, one practical effect of
this new approach is to make it critical that local officials know
what items are scheduled to come before them at least 30 days in
advance.  Only in this way can they discuss with their local
attorney whether they should seek formal advice from the Commission
staff, actually make that request, and hope to have a response

before the item is presented before them. This advance notice will
be particularly critical if a hearing is time-sensitive and cannot
be continued to await the receipt of a Commission advice letter.
The only other option is, of course, abstaining as a "preventative"
measure--something that is usually anathema to local decision-
makers.

Any member of any City board, commission, committee, or task
force that is subject to conflict of interest rules should feel free
to ask questions about the new FPPC rule or any other conflict of
interest provisions. The staff member assigned to your body is an
excellent resource on these questions. If you believe it would be a
good use of your time and helpful to your work, that staff member
can also arrange to have an appropriate attorney in my office
attend one of your meetings to address the conflict of interest
issues that are most relevant to you and the issues you deal with
most frequently.

[Signature: Alice C. Graff]

Alice C. Graff
City Attorney

ACG:cj
CONFLICT.MEM


-2-
